headline: Chips Lead a Risk-On Finish as Quarter Closes on a High Note
body_html:
Wall Street wrapped the second quarter of 2026 with a decisive tilt toward technology hardware, as semiconductors surged 4.79% to claim the session's top sector spot. The move capped what multiple observers are calling the strongest quarter for U.S. equities since 2020, a run forged against the backdrop of an ongoing conflict with Iran and an artificial-intelligence spending cycle that continues to pull capital into the chip supply chain. Breadth was constructive, with 112 names advancing against 84 declining across the 198-stock universe tracked by this desk.

The rotation story was equally telling. Energy and Industrials followed Semiconductors higher, gaining 1.81% and 1.39% respectively, while defensive corners of the market absorbed the selling. Healthcare dropped 0.98% and Consumer fell 0.66%, signaling that portfolio managers used the quarter-end session to trim lower-growth, rate-sensitive positions in favor of cyclical and capital-expenditure-linked names.

Sandisk led all movers, surging 10.89%, with Credo Technology close behind at 10.69% — a reminder that memory and connectivity subsectors remain the sharp edge of the semiconductor trade. On the downside, Stryker shed 4.74%, the steepest decline in the universe, as medical-device names broadly underperformed. As the first half closes, the market's message is clear: capital is still chasing the infrastructure of the AI era.

briefs.headline: Bloom Energy Lights Up on Fuel-Cell Demand Optimism
briefs.body: Bloom Energy climbed 10.07% on the session, making it the standout gainer outside the semiconductor complex. The fuel-cell specialist has benefited from growing interest in distributed power solutions tied to data-center energy demand, and Tuesday's move suggests investors see that thesis deepening as AI infrastructure buildouts accelerate.

briefs.headline: Vertiv Extends Data-Center Infrastructure Rally
briefs.body: Vertiv Holdings advanced 9.07%, continuing a strong run for companies that supply critical power and thermal management equipment to data centers. The gain underscores the breadth of the AI infrastructure trade beyond chip designers, as operators race to expand capacity in response to surging compute demand.

briefs.headline: Iran Diplomatic Impasse Keeps Geopolitical Risk Premium Alive
briefs.body: Tehran's refusal to engage with U.S. envoys dimmed hopes for a near-term ceasefire agreement, keeping geopolitical uncertainty in the background even as markets posted strong quarterly gains. Analysts noted the situation in occupied southern Lebanon remains fluid, with Israel signaling it has no immediate plans to withdraw forces from the region.

briefs.headline: Best Quarter for U.S. Stocks Since 2020 Draws to a Close
briefs.body: The S&P 500 and Nasdaq finished the second quarter with their strongest three-month performance in six years, a remarkable outcome given the Iran conflict and elevated interest-rate uncertainty. The AI investment cycle was widely credited as the primary driver, concentrating gains in hardware, connectivity, and power infrastructure stocks.

briefs.headline: Healthcare Devices Bear the Brunt of Quarter-End Rotation
briefs.body: Stryker fell 4.74% and DexCom declined 3.76%, making the healthcare-devices subsector among the session's most notable laggards. The broad 0.98% drop in Healthcare suggests active reallocation rather than company-specific news, as fund managers rotated out of defensive positions ahead of the new quarter.

briefs.headline: Consumer and Homebuilder Names Slide as Risk Appetite Shifts
briefs.body: Williams-Sonoma shed 2.94% and Hershey lost 3.38%, while the Consumer sector as a whole fell 0.66% and Homebuilders dipped 0.58%. The pattern reflects investor caution around rate-sensitive and discretionary spending names at a moment when capital is gravitating toward technology and energy infrastructure plays.
